summary: Spolsky warns independent developers against building applications that merely fill gaps in a platform vendor's product offering. He argues that platform vendors will inevitably add missing features themselves, destroying any business built around those gaps. Using the iPhone as an example, he notes that when only 1% of users pay for a gap-filling utility, the vendor sees the problem as unsolved for the remaining 99% and will address it directly. Instead, developers should build vertical applications targeting specific niches that the platform vendor would never consider core features.
key_points: Building apps that fill holes in a platform vendor's product is inherently risky because the vendor will eventually fill those holes themselves
Platform vendors see gap-filling third-party apps as evidence the problem remains unsolved for the vast majority of their users
The safe opportunity on any platform is vertical applications serving specific audiences the vendor will never target
Only a tiny percentage of platform users will pay for utility add-ons, which motivates the vendor to build the feature natively
money_quotes: Filling little gaps in another company's product lineup is snatching nickels from the path of an oncoming steam roller.
They will solve it, if it's really a problem. And you'll be out of business.
Sometimes developers choose a niche that's either directly in the path of the vendor, or even worse, on the roadmap of the vendor. In those cases, they don't really deserve our sympathy.
There is exactly zero chance that Apple is ever going to add a feature to the iPhone for dentists. Zero.
With any kind of add-on, selling to 1% of the platform is a huge success.
A good platform always has opportunities for applications that aren't just gap-fillers.
key_insight: Developers building on a platform should pursue vertical applications for specific audiences rather than gap-filling utilities that the platform vendor will inevitably absorb.
